text: This section defines the entry and exit criteria for user acceptance testing (UAT) and production readiness per functional module. Each criterion is measurable, traceable, and aligned with NDI operational requirements. All criteria must be satisfied before module promotion from UAT to PRE-PROD.

1. Functional Completeness 360° asset view aggregates metadata from catalog, DQ scores, DLP incidents, NDI evidence, training status, workflow history, and Open Data candidate status in single page. Asset search supports full-text Arabic and English queries across 1 million assets with results in < 500ms (p95). Governance status accurately reflects real-time sync from catalog (lag < 5 minutes). Classification override workflow triggers when catalog classification conflicts with DGOP manual classification. Business glossary terms visible in Asset 360° with definition, acronyms, synonyms, related terms, and approval status. Data profiling results (column statistics, pattern analysis, anomaly detection) visible in Asset 360° for profiled assets. MDM golden record status, match confidence, and survivorship history visible for master data entities. Privacy by Design status (SDLC phase-gate completion, DPIA requirement, privacy controls) visible per asset. 2. Performance Asset 360° page loads in < 2 seconds for assets with 50+ related workflow cases and 20+ evidence files. Bulk asset import (CSV/Excel) processes 10,000 assets in < 10 minutes with validation error report. 3. Compliance All asset metadata changes trigger automatic NDI gap analysis and update compliance score within 15 minutes. Personal data flag accuracy ≥ 99% compared to DLP scan results and catalog metadata. 4-level classification (Public, Internal, Restricted, Confidential) displayed with control enforcement indicators.

1. Functional Completeness All 18 governance workflow types deployable via BPMN 2.0: Owner Assignment, Steward Assignment, Metadata Certification, DQ Remediation, DLP Incident Review, Data Sharing Request, Open Data Candidate Review, FOI Request Review, FOI Appeal, Personal Data Review, Classification Approval, Access Review, NDI Evidence Review, Steward Assessment, Charter Approval, Policy Lifecycle, Glossary Term Approval, MDM Match Resolution. SLA timer events calculate KSA business days correctly (excluding Friday/Saturday and public holidays). Escalation triggers automatically at 80% SLA threshold and breach notification sent to DMO + executive. 4-level governance escalation path: Domain Council → Data Stewardship Council → Data Governance Board → Executive Steering Committee with DSP-recommended timeframes (30/14/7/5 days for policy/BDE/DQ/access decisions). Workflow task routing supports dynamic assignment based on stewardship registry, workload balancing, and backup delegation. Appeals workflow supports independent reviewer assignment, 10-day appeal window, and decision override with full audit trail. Severity-based SLA framework: Critical (4h triage / 8h assignment / 5d resolution), High (2d triage / 5d assignment / 15d resolution), Medium (5d triage / 10d assignment / 30d resolution), Low (5d triage / 10d assignment / 60d resolution). 2. Performance Workflow engine processes 1,000 concurrent cases with < 1s task assignment latency. Timer job execution accuracy: 100% of timers fire within ±30 seconds of scheduled time. Process state recovery from Zeebe event log completes in < 5 minutes after engine restart. 3. Operational Resilience Zero workflow data loss during pod restart or node failure (event sourcing persistence verified). Dead-letter queue processes failed service tasks with 3 retries and 5s exponential backoff; manual intervention alert after exhaustion.

1. Functional Completeness Open Data Candidate Registry automatically identifies candidates from asset catalog using ML-based classification (PUBLIC, no PII, DQ ≥ 80, metadata ≥ 90%). Assessment wizard executes 8 automated checks (classification, PII, DQ, metadata, DLP, legal, aggregation, anonymization) and generates eligibility score. Publication workflow routes through Business Steward → Owner → Privacy Officer → Security Reviewer → DQ Steward → ODIAO with SLA tracking at each stage. Review scheduler triggers quarterly/semi-annual/annual reviews based on publication frequency. Usage monitoring ingests portal statistics (downloads, API calls, unique users) via webhook with daily aggregation. 2. Integration Published dataset metadata and status synchronized to National Open Data Portal within 10 minutes of ODIAO approval. Retirement workflow automatically triggers when asset classification changes from PUBLIC or owner requests deprecation. 3. Compliance 100% of published datasets have complete evidence chain: assessment → approval → publication → review → retirement/update. Open Data KPIs (candidate conversion rate, publication SLA, usage growth) calculate accurately and feed executive dashboard.

compliance ≥ 90% (requests closed within statutory deadline / total requests). All FOI decisions, redactions, and appeals maintain tamper-evident audit log with chain of custody. Breach notification workflow triggers automatically if SLA breach detected, escalating to DMO and legal within 1 hour.

1. Functional Completeness 6-dimension DQ scoring implemented: Accuracy, Completeness, Consistency, Timeliness, Validity, Uniqueness — each with configurable thresholds, measurement methods, and scoring algorithms. Per-dimension rule types operational: Range check, Format validation, Referential integrity, Cross-field validation, Business derivation, Completeness check, Duplicate detection, Timeliness check. Hierarchical scorecards: Overall score → Dimension scores → Domain scores → Asset-level scores with drill-down navigation. DQ dimension dashboard displays real-time scores with trend indicators (7-day, 30-day, 90-day) and threshold breach alerts. Severity-based SLA framework: Critical (4h triage / 8h assignment / 5d resolution), High (2d triage / 5d assignment / 15d resolution), Medium (5d triage / 10d assignment / 30d resolution), Low (5d triage / 10d assignment / 60d resolution). 4-level escalation path: Domain Council → Data Stewardship Council → Data Governance Board → Executive Steering Committee. RCA template library: 5 Whys, Fishbone Diagram, Process Mapping, Data Lineage Analysis — with guided investigation workflow and technique selection. Rule lifecycle management: rule definition → review → approve → deploy → monitor → retire workflow with approval gates. DQ scorecard export to PDF/Excel with executive summary, dimension breakdown, trend charts, and remediation recommendations. 2. Accuracy DQ score calculation accuracy: 100% match between manual verification and system output for sample of 100 assets across all 6 dimensions. Dimension threshold breach detection: 100% of threshold violations flagged within 1 hour of rule execution. Escalation routing accuracy: 100% of escalated issues routed to correct governance body per escalation matrix. 3. Performance DQ scorecard refresh completes in < 3 seconds for 10,000 assets with 6 dimensions. Batch dimension recalculation for 100,000 assets completes in < 15 minutes. Scorecard drill-down from overall to asset-level loads in < 2 seconds. 4. Integration DQ dimensional scores visible in Asset 360° view with per-dimension breakdown. Scores synchronized to data catalog within 5 minutes of recalculation. Kafka events emitted for all threshold breaches, score changes, and escalation triggers. Integration with external DQ tools for rule execution and result ingestion.

1. Functional Completeness 6 masking techniques implemented: Static Masking: Permanent irreversible masking for non-production environments; consistent masking preserves referential integrity across related tables. Dynamic Masking: Real-time policy-based masking for production queries; different users see different masked views based on role and clearance. Tokenization: Reversible substitution using token vault; format-preserving token generation (FPE-FF1). Anonymization: Irreversible transformation for analytics datasets; k-anonymity (k≥5) and l-diversity validation. Pseudonymization: Reversible identifier replacement using key vault; suitable for GDPR/PDPL compliance scenarios. Redaction: Full or partial character replacement (e.g., XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-1234 for credit card numbers). Masking policy designer: define policies per classification level, data subject, and environment; apply policies to database columns, file fields, and API responses. Automatic masking policy suggestions based on data profiling results and classification labels. Masking audit log: all masking/unmasking operations logged with user, timestamp, policy applied, and justification. Masking validation: verify referential integrity preserved after static masking; verify k-anonymity achieved after anonymization. 2. Security Masking keys stored in HSM; token vault encrypted with AES-256-GCM. Dynamic masking policies evaluated via ABAC; no user can bypass masking without explicit override authorization. Static masking irreversible: no back-door or recovery mechanism for production-masked data. Masking policy changes require dual approval from Security Officer and DPO. 3. Performance Dynamic masking overhead < 10ms per query for policies with ≤ 10 field rules. Static masking for 1 million rows completes in < 30 minutes. Tokenization/de-tokenization latency < 5ms per value via vault cache. 4. Integration Masking policies linked to 4-level classification scheme: automatic technique selection per classification level. Integration with DEV/TEST/UAT environment provisioning for automatic data masking. API for external systems to request masked/unmasked data with authorization check.

1. Functional Completeness 4-level classification scheme operational: PUBLIC, INTERNAL, RESTRICTED, CONFIDENTIAL — each with specific controls: PUBLIC: No access restrictions; minimal logging INTERNAL: RBAC role requirement; standard audit logging RESTRICTED: RBAC + need-to-know justification; enhanced audit logging; MFA required for admin CONFIDENTIAL: RBAC + ABAC dynamic authorization; MFA mandatory; field-level encryption; enhanced audit; watermarked downloads ABAC Policy Administration Point (PAP): policy creation, editing, testing, and versioning with policy conflict detection. ABAC Policy Decision Point (PDP): real-time policy evaluation with attribute resolution from identity provider, HR system, and device management. ABAC Policy Enforcement Point (PEP): intercepts all data access requests and enforces PDP decisions with < 100ms latency. Attribute management: user attributes (role, department, clearance, location), data attributes (classification, domain, sensitivity tags), environmental attributes (time, location, device trust level). Policy simulation tool: test ABAC policies against hypothetical access scenarios before deployment. Classification workflow: automatic classification based on content analysis, manual classification by data owner, classification review and override workflow. Classification propagation: classification inherited from parent domain, with override capability at asset level. 2. Accuracy ABAC policy evaluation accuracy: 100% correct decisions for 1,000 test scenarios including edge cases and attribute combinations. Policy conflict detection: 100% of conflicting policies flagged before deployment. Classification accuracy: automatic classification ≥ 95% accuracy validated against manual expert classification of 200 assets. 3. Performance PDP authorization decision latency < 50ms (p50), < 100ms (p95). Policy deployment propagation < 30 seconds across all enforcement points. Classification workflow completion < 2 business days from submission to approval. 4. Integration ABAC attributes synchronized from Nafath (identity), entity HR system (department, role), and MDM (organizational hierarchy). Classification labels synchronized to data catalog, DQ tool, and DLP tool. ABAC audit events forwarded to SIEM in real-time.

1. Functional Completeness 7 Privacy by Design principles mapped to SDLC phases: Proactive not Reactive (Requirements phase) Privacy as Default Setting (Design phase) Privacy Embedded into Design (Development phase) Full Functionality (Testing phase) End-to-End Security (Deployment phase) Visibility and Transparency (All phases) Respect for User Privacy (All phases) SDLC phase-gate checklist for each of 5 phases: Requirements, Design, Development, Testing, Deployment. Requirements phase gate: DPIA trigger assessment, data subject identification, processing purpose validation, legal basis documentation, consent requirement flag. Design phase gate: data minimization review, pseudonymization design, encryption specification, access control design, retention period definition. Development phase gate: privacy control implementation verification, PII field encryption, audit log integration, API security review. Testing phase gate: privacy functional testing, penetration testing for PII exposure, DLP validation, consent mechanism testing. Deployment phase gate: privacy configuration verification, monitoring setup, breach response procedure confirmation, privacy documentation completeness. Automatic DPIA creation when data subject = sensitive personal data or processing activity = high risk. Privacy risk scoring per project: cumulative score from phase-gate results with Red/Amber/Green status. Integration with SDLC tools (Jira, Azure DevOps, GitLab) for phase-gate checklist injection and approval blocking. 2. Accuracy Phase-gate checklist completion accuracy: 100% of required gates triggered for registered projects. DPIA auto-creation accuracy: 100% of high-risk projects identified and DPIA workflow initiated. Privacy risk score calculation: 100% match between manual assessment and system output for sample of 20 projects. 3. Performance Phase-gate checklist load time < 1 second. Privacy risk score recalculation < 5 seconds after phase-gate completion. SDLC integration event processing latency < 30 seconds from external tool webhook. 4. Integration Webhook receivers for Jira, Azure DevOps, GitLab project lifecycle events. Privacy risk scores visible in project dashboard and DGOP executive dashboard. DPIA workflows linked to Privacy & Data Protection module. SDLC gate completion events published to Kafka for audit trail.

compliance task scheduler with configurable frequencies: daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, semi-annual, annual. 1. Functional Completeness Charter Management module with template library containing 8 mandatory elements: Purpose and Scope Governance Authority and Mandate Governance Bodies and Roles Decision Rights Matrix Escalation Paths and Timeframes Data Domain Definitions Review Cycle and Amendment Process Metrics and Success Criteria Charter approval workflow: DMO draft → Legal review → DGSC review → Executive approval → Publication. Charter version control with immutable history, effective dates, and deprecation tracking. Charter periodic review trigger: automatic review task created per configured review cycle (default: annual). Policy Lifecycle Management supports 5-level hierarchy: Level 1: Enterprise Policy (Board/DGSC approved) Level 2: Domain Policy (Data Council approved) Level 3: Standard (Working Group approved) Level 4: Procedure (DMO approved) Level 5: Guideline (DMO published, advisory) Policy workflow per level: draft → stakeholder review → approval → publish → distribute → monitor → review → retire. Policy dependency mapping: track relationships between policies, standards, and procedures. Policy compliance linkage: link policies to NDI specifications, DQ rules, and access controls for compliance reporting. Policy acknowledgement tracking: require stakeholders to acknowledge policy updates with digital signature equivalent. Decision rights matrix with time-boxed escalation: 30 days for enterprise policies, 14 days for BDE decisions, 7 days for DQ resolutions, 5 days for access requests. 2. Integration Charter and policy versions linked to NDI Compliance Hub as evidence. Policy lifecycle events published to Kafka for audit trail and notification. Policy acknowledgement status integrated with training compliance KPI. Decision rights matrix embedded in workflow engine escalation configuration. 3. Compliance 100% of active policies have current version, approved status, and complete evidence chain. Policy review completion rate ≥ 95% within configured review cycle. Charter review triggered 30 days before anniversary date. 4. ## 3.21 Enhanced Search — Acceptance Criteria (v4.1 NEW)

Scenario 1: Cross-Object Search Preconditions: DGOP instance populated with ≥ 500 assets, 50 workflows, 100 glossary terms, 20 policies, 30 stewards, and 200 evidence files across multiple domains. Steps: * Enter search term “customer” in global search bar from homepage. * Execute search and review results grouped by object type. * Verify results include: data assets (customer master dataset, customer demographic report), workflows (Customer Data Steward Assignment workflow cases), glossary terms (“Customer” business definition, “Customer Segment” related term), stewards (Customer Domain Business Steward profile), and policies (Customer Data Handling Policy). * Verify each result displays contextual snippet with “customer” highlighted in bold. * Verify result count displayed per object type tab. * Click each object type tab and verify relevant results appear. * Verify results sorted by relevance score (highest first) with object type boost applied. Expected Results: Search returns results across all object types containing “customer”. Results grouped by type with counts. Snippets show highlighted matching terms. Cross-object discovery enables comprehensive governance context. Success Criteria: Results span ≥ 5 object types; relevance ranking present; snippet highlighting accurate; result count matches actual results; response time < 300ms (p95).

Scenario 2: Faceted Search Filtering Preconditions: Search index populated with ≥ 1,000 objects across ≥ 5 domains with mixed classification levels. Steps: * Execute broad search term “report” and note total result count. * Apply Domain filter: select “Finance” domain; verify result count narrows to Finance domain objects only. * Add Classification filter: select “INTERNAL”; verify result count further narrows to Finance + INTERNAL objects. * Add Date Range filter: select “Last 30 days”; verify result count narrows to Finance + INTERNAL + last 30 days. * Add Object Type filter: select “Data Assets” only; verify only assets matching all filters displayed. * Remove Date Range filter; verify result count increases accordingly. * Clear all filters; verify original result count restored. * Verify facet counts update dynamically as each filter is applied. Expected Results: Each applied filter narrows result set correctly. Filter combination works with AND logic. Facet counts update dynamically. Clear filters restores full result set. Success Criteria: Filter accuracy 100%; facet count update < 200ms; result narrowing logical; clear filters functional; no orphaned results after filter removal.

Scenario 3: Arabic Search with RTL Handling Preconditions: Search index contains Arabic content including asset names, glossary terms, steward names, and policy titles in Arabic. Steps: * Switch interface to Arabic mode (RTL layout). * Enter Arabic search query: “العميل” (customer) in search bar. * Verify search bar renders correctly in RTL with right-aligned text input. * Execute search and verify bilingual results: Arabic content ranked with Arabic match boost, English content with Arabic translations included. * Verify result snippets display Arabic text with proper RTL rendering and highlighted matching terms. * Verify search suggestions dropdown appears with Arabic suggestions when typing Arabic text. * Enter mixed-script query: “عميل customer” and verify results from both Arabic and English content. * Verify no text truncation, overlap, or layout breakage in RTL search results page. Expected Results: Arabic search executes successfully with RTL rendering. Bilingual results returned. Mixed-script queries handled. Layout correct in RTL mode. Success Criteria: Arabic query response < 300ms; RTL rendering correct; bilingual results ≥ 80% relevance; no layout defects; mixed-script query functional.

Scenario 4: Saved Search & Alert Preconditions: User has saved search capability enabled. ≥ 5 assets exist in “Health” domain with classification “INTERNAL”. Steps: * Execute search with query “insurance” + Domain filter “Health” + Classification “INTERNAL”. * Verify results show matching assets. * Click “Save Search” button; enter name “Health Insurance Assets”; set sharing to “Private”; save. * Verify saved search appears in user’s “My Saved Searches” list with search criteria summary. * Create new asset named “Health Insurance Claims 2025” in Health domain with INTERNAL classification. * Wait up to 5 minutes for alert evaluation cycle. * Verify user receives notification (in-app bell + email) that new result matches saved search “Health Insurance Assets”. * Click notification; verify redirected to search results page showing new asset. * Delete saved search; verify no further alerts received. Expected Results: Saved search created successfully. Alert triggers on new matching asset. Notification delivered with link to results. Alert stops after saved search deletion. Success Criteria: Saved search creation 100%; alert trigger ≤ 5 min; notification delivery 100%; link navigation accurate; deletion stops alerts.

Scenario 5: Search Access Control (ABAC Enforcement) Preconditions: Two test users configured: User A (standard steward, Finance domain access, INTERNAL classification max) and User B (senior steward, Finance + HR domain access, RESTRICTED classification max). Same search query executed by both users. Steps: * User A searches for “salary”: verify results only from Finance domain with classification ≤ INTERNAL. No HR domain results. No RESTRICTED/CONFIDENTIAL results. * User B searches for “salary”: verify results from Finance AND HR domains with classification ≤ RESTRICTED. No CONFIDENTIAL results. * Verify User B sees strictly more results than User A for same query. * Verify neither user sees any indication of hidden results (no “X results hidden” message). * Verify search audit log records both queries with user IDs, result counts, and filters applied. * Verify CONFIDENTIAL asset containing “salary” appears for neither user. Expected Results: User B sees more results than User A due to broader domain and classification access. No unauthorized result leakage. Audit log captures both queries. No indication of hidden results. Success Criteria: ABAC filtering accuracy 100%; result count difference explained by permissions; no unauthorized access; audit log complete; no information leakage.
4.14 Error Handling & Notifications — UAT Scenarios (v4.1 NEW)

Scenario 1: Form Validation Errors — Bilingual Error Display Preconditions: Access to 5 different input screens across DGOP modules (Administration, Ownership & Stewardship Registry, Workflow, Asset Hub, NDI Compliance). Steps: * Screen 1 — Administration Domain Configuration: Submit form with empty required field “Domain Name” and invalid email format in “Contact Email”. Verify inline error appears below each field in Arabic and English. Verify error describes the problem and suggests correction (e.g., “Domain Name is required. Please enter a unique domain name. / اسم المجال مطلوب. يرجى إدخال اسم مجال فريد.”). * Screen 2 — Steward Assignment: Submit assignment with non-existent user ID and overlapping effective dates. Verify inline error for invalid user and business rule violation for date overlap with specific guidance. * Screen 3 — Workflow Task Completion: Submit task form with evidence file exceeding 50MB limit and unsupported file type (.exe). Verify file validation errors with size limit and allowed formats listed. * Screen 4 — Asset Metadata Edit: Submit asset with duplicate business name already assigned to another asset. Verify uniqueness violation error with link to existing asset. * Screen 5 — NDI Evidence Upload: Submit evidence upload with missing required description and future-dated evidence date. Verify required field error and date validation error with acceptable range. * For each screen: verify errors appear on blur (real-time) before submit; verify Arabic toggle displays correct RTL error messages; verify error clears when field corrected. Expected Results: All 5 screens display clear bilingual errors with corrective guidance. Real-time validation works. Arabic errors render correctly in RTL. Success Criteria: Error display 100% on all 5 screens; bilingual accuracy 100%; corrective guidance present; real-time validation < 500ms; RTL rendering correct.

Scenario 2: API Error Display — RFC 7807 Problem Details Preconditions: API testing environment with ability to simulate failures. Postman or equivalent API client. Steps: * API Call 1 — Authentication Failure: Send API request with expired JWT token. Verify response returns RFC 7807 format: { "type": "/errors/auth/token-expired", "title": "Authentication Failed", "status": 401, "detail": "Your session has expired. Please re-authenticate via Nafath SSO.", "instance": "/api/v1/assets/12345" }. Verify correlation ID present in response header X-Correlation-Id. * API Call 2 — Validation Failure: Send POST request to create asset with invalid classification level. Verify RFC 7807 response with validation details extension: { "type": "/errors/validation/invalid-classification", "title": "Validation Error", "status": 400, "detail": "Classification level 'TOP-SECRET' is not valid. Allowed values: PUBLIC, INTERNAL, RESTRICTED, CONFIDENTIAL.", "instance": "/api/v1/assets", "invalidParams": [{"field": "classification", "rejectedValue": "TOP-SECRET", "message": "Invalid classification level"}] }. * API Call 3 — Authorization Failure: Send API request to access CONFIDENTIAL asset with standard steward credentials. Verify RFC 7807 response: { "type": "/errors/auth/insufficient-privileges", "title": "Access Denied", "status": 403, "detail": "You do not have sufficient privileges to access CONFIDENTIAL assets. Required role: Security Reviewer or higher.", "instance": "/api/v1/assets/CONF-001" }. * API Call 4 — System Error: Send API request that triggers internal server error (simulate via test endpoint). Verify RFC 7807 response with generic user message and correlation ID: { "type": "/errors/system/internal-error", "title": "System Error", "status": 500, "detail": "An unexpected error occurred. Please contact support with correlation ID: abc-123-def.", "instance": "/api/v1/reports/generate" }. Verify correlation ID displayed to user matches log entry. * API Call 5 — Rate Limiting: Send excessive API requests to trigger rate limit. Verify RFC 7807 response: { "type": "/errors/system/rate-limited", "title": "Rate Limit Exceeded", "status": 429, "detail": "You have exceeded the allowed request rate. Please wait 60 seconds before retrying.", "instance": "/api/v1/search", "retryAfter": 60 }. * For each API call: verify correlation ID in response header; verify content-type application/problem+json; verify all mandatory RFC 7807 fields present. Expected Results: All 5 API errors return valid RFC 7807 Problem Details. Correlation IDs present. Bilingual support verified. Error classification accurate. Success Criteria: RFC 7807 compliance 100%; correlation ID present 100%; mandatory fields complete; bilingual support; content-type correct.

Scenario 3: Notification Delivery — Multi-Channel Verification Preconditions: Test user configured with access to in-app, email, SMS, and push notification channels. 5 different notification-triggering events configured. Steps: * Event 1 — Workflow Assignment: Assign task to test user; verify in-app notification appears within 10 seconds; verify email received with bilingual subject and task details; verify SMS received with brief alert (if configured for HIGH urgency). * Event 2 — SLA Breach Warning: Trigger workflow approaching 80% SLA threshold; verify in-app toast notification with amber warning color; verify email received with breach warning and recommended action; verify escalation notification sent to manager. * Event 3 — Training Reminder: Trigger training deadline approaching (7 days); verify in-app notification with training course name and deadline; verify email with direct enrollment link; verify no SMS (MEDIUM urgency). * Event 4 — Community Mention: Mention test user in forum post; verify in-app notification with post snippet and reply button; verify email with link to forum thread. * Event 5 — System Maintenance: Schedule system maintenance window; verify mandatory broadcast notification received in-app (cannot be dismissed until read); verify email received; verify push notification received. * For each event: verify notification content is bilingual; verify notification template variables correctly substituted; verify action links navigate to correct screens. Expected Results: All 5 notification types delivered across configured channels. Bilingual content accurate. Delivery within SLA. Templates correctly rendered. Success Criteria: In-app delivery < 10s; email delivery < 2 min; SMS delivery < 2 min; bilingual accuracy 100%; template rendering correct; action links functional.

Scenario 4: Notification Preferences — Custom Configuration Preconditions: Test user account with default notification preferences. Steps: * Navigate to Notification Preferences screen. * Disable SMS channel entirely; verify SMS removed from all notification type configurations. * For “Workflow” category: enable in-app + email only; disable SMS and push. * For “Training” category: enable email digest mode (daily); disable in-app, SMS, and push. * For “Community” category: enable in-app + push only; disable email and SMS. * Set quiet hours: 20:00–06:00 KSA time; verify notifications during quiet hours queued for delivery after window ends (except CRITICAL). * Save preferences; verify confirmation message displayed. * Trigger workflow assignment: verify in-app + email received, NO SMS. * Trigger training reminder: verify daily digest email received (not immediate), NO in-app. * Trigger community mention: verify in-app + push received, NO email. * Simulate CRITICAL security alert during quiet hours: verify immediate delivery bypasses quiet hours. * Revert preferences to defaults; verify default profile restored. Expected Results: Only selected channels receive notifications per category. Quiet hours respected except CRITICAL. Digest mode functions. Revert to defaults works. Success Criteria: Preference filtering 100%; quiet hours respected; CRITICAL bypass works; digest mode functional; revert restores defaults.

Scenario 5: Error Escalation — Auto-Ticket Creation & Dashboard Preconditions: System error simulation endpoint available. ITSM integration configured. Error analytics dashboard accessible. Steps: * Simulate P1 system error: trigger database connection failure on asset search API. * Verify error page displays to user with generic message and correlation ID. * Verify correlation ID format: UUID v4, 36 characters with hyphens. * Within 2 minutes: verify auto-created support ticket in ITSM with correlation ID, error classification (SYSTEM_ERROR), affected module (Asset Hub), timestamp, and user context. * Verify L2 support receives immediate alert (P1 escalation) with error details and correlation ID. * Navigate to Error Analytics Dashboard; verify new error appears in “Recent Critical Errors” table. * Verify dashboard metrics update: total error count incremented, system error category count incremented, affected module heat map updated. * Verify “Most Common Errors” table updates within 15 minutes with new error entry. * Simulate 5 more instances of same error; verify error trending graph shows spike. * Verify automated threshold alert sent to L2 when error rate exceeds 50% of baseline. * Resolve simulated error; verify dashboard reflects resolution status. Expected Results: P1 error auto-creates ticket within 2 minutes. Correlation ID links user error to support ticket. Dashboard updates in real-time. Threshold alerting functional. Success Criteria: Auto-ticket creation ≤ 2 min; correlation ID accuracy 100%; dashboard update < 5 min; threshold alert fires correctly; error trend accurate.
4.15 Business Process & Workflow Management — UAT Scenarios (v4.1 NEW)

Scenario 1: Visual Workflow Designer — Custom Approval Workflow Preconditions: Workflow designer accessible. User has WORKFLOW_DESIGNER role. BPMN validation tools available. Steps: * Open Visual Workflow Designer; create new workflow “Data Product Approval v1.0”. * Drag and drop 5 node types: (1) Start Event, (2) User Task “Business Steward Review” with form assignment, (3) Exclusive Gateway “Approved?” with condition expressions, (4) User Task “Technical Review” (approved branch), (5) End Event. * Configure Business Steward Review task: assign to “Business Data Steward” role, set 3-day SLA, attach evidence requirement (mandatory approval comment), configure notification trigger. * Configure Exclusive Gateway: approved condition ${approvalDecision == 'APPROVED'}, rejected condition ${approvalDecision == 'REJECTED'} routing to rejection end event. * Configure Technical Review task: assign to “Technical Data Steward” role, set 2-day SLA, attach technical checklist form. * Add parallel gateway for dual approval: route to both Security Reviewer and DQ Steward simultaneously; both must approve before proceeding to final End Event. * Execute workflow validation; verify zero validation errors. * Save workflow as draft; verify version 1.0 draft created. * Export workflow to BPMN 2.0 XML; validate using external BPMN validator; verify valid BPMN 2.0 XML produced. * Import exported BPMN into fresh workflow; verify all nodes, configurations, and properties preserved. Expected Results: Custom workflow designed with 5+ node types. Validation passes. BPMN export valid and import preserves all configurations. Parallel gateway for dual approval configured. Success Criteria: BPMN validation passes; export produces valid BPMN 2.0 XML; import preserves 100% of configuration; parallel gateway functional; SLA timers configured.

Scenario 2: Workflow Versioning — Draft, Publish, and Instance Migration Preconditions: Existing workflow “Metadata Certification” with published v1.0 and 5 active running instances. Steps: * Verify v1.0 published with 5 active instances running. * Create v1.1 draft: modify SLA from 5 days to 3 days; add new mandatory evidence field “Quality Assessment Report”; update notification template. * Execute workflow simulation on v1.1: run 3 test instances through happy path, rejection path, and escalation path; verify all paths execute correctly. * Publish v1.1; verify v1.0 remains active for existing 5 instances. * Start 3 new workflow instances; verify they execute on v1.1 (3-day SLA, new evidence requirement). * Verify original 5 instances continue on v1.0 (5-day SLA, no new evidence requirement). * Allow 2 original v1.0 instances to complete; verify they complete successfully on v1.0. * Attempt to edit v1.0; verify system prevents editing published version with warning. * Archive v1.0 after all instances complete; verify v1.0 status changes to ARCHIVED. * Verify version history page shows complete lineage: v1.0 (published → archived), v1.1 (published → current). Expected Results: v1.0 instances complete on original version. New instances use v1.1. Version history accurate. Archived workflows immutable. Simulation validated before publish. Success Criteria: Version isolation 100%; new instances on latest version; existing instances complete on original; archive functional; simulation passes; history accurate.

Scenario 3: Dynamic Task Routing — Workload-Based Assignment Preconditions: 3 Business Stewards configured in Finance domain with different current workloads: Steward A (2 active tasks), Steward B (7 active tasks), Steward C (5 active tasks). Workload-based routing enabled. Steps: * Configure workflow to use workload-based routing with “least-loaded” strategy. * Initiate 6 new workflow instances requiring Business Steward assignment. * Verify first task routes to Steward A (least loaded with 2 tasks). * Verify second task routes to Steward A (now 3 tasks, still least or tied). * Continue until Steward A reaches 6 tasks; verify subsequent tasks route to least-loaded steward. * Simulate Steward B setting out-of-office status. * Initiate new workflow instance; verify task routes to available steward (not Steward B). * Remove out-of-office status for Steward B; verify new task can route to Steward B again. * Verify workload dashboard updates in real-time with task count changes. * Disable workload-based routing; revert to round-robin; verify tasks distribute evenly regardless of workload. Expected Results: Tasks route to least-loaded steward. Out-of-office respected. Dashboard reflects real-time workload. Routing strategy change takes effect immediately. Success Criteria: Workload routing accuracy 100%; out-of-office exclusion 100%; dashboard update < 30s; strategy switch functional; assignment balanced.

Scenario 4: Parallel Approval — Three-Branch Approval Preconditions: Workflow configured with parallel gateway splitting to 3 approval branches: Business Steward, Security Reviewer, and DQ Steward. Unanimous approval required. Steps: * Initiate workflow instance requiring triple approval. * Verify 3 parallel tasks created simultaneously: Task 1 (Business Steward), Task 2 (Security Reviewer), Task 3 (DQ Steward). * Verify all 3 assignees receive notification within 10 seconds. * Approve Task 1 (Business Steward); verify workflow waits for remaining 2 approvals. * Approve Task 2 (Security Reviewer); verify workflow still waits for Task 3. * Approve Task 3 (DQ Steward); verify workflow proceeds to next stage only after all 3 approvals. * Verify parallel gateway join condition satisfied (all 3 approvals received). * Initiate second instance; approve 2 of 3 tasks; have third reviewer reject. * Verify workflow routes to rejection end event (unanimous approval not achieved). * Verify audit trail shows all approval actions with timestamps and approver identities. Expected Results: All 3 approvals required before progression. Single rejection blocks progression. Notifications delivered to all approvers. Audit trail complete. Success Criteria: Parallel task creation 100%; unanimous approval enforced; single rejection blocks; notifications < 10s; audit trail complete.

Scenario 5: SLA Enforcement — 48-Hour SLA with Escalation Preconditions: Workflow configured with 48-hour SLA timer. Escalation configured at 80% (38.4 hours) and breach at 100% (48 hours). KSA business day calendar configured (excluding Friday/Saturday). Steps: * Initiate workflow instance at 09:00 Sunday KSA time. * Verify SLA timer calculates correctly: 48 KSA business hours = 6 business days (Sunday–Thursday × 2 weeks minus Friday/Saturday), due 09:00 following Thursday. * At 80% SLA (38.4 hours elapsed, approximately following Wednesday 15:24): verify amber warning notification sent to assignee and manager. * Allow SLA to reach 100% (48 hours, 09:00 Thursday): verify red breach notification sent to assignee, manager, and DMO. * Verify escalation workflow triggered: task reassigned to manager with CRITICAL priority. * Verify SLA breach recorded in workflow dashboard with red status indicator. * Verify KSA public holidays excluded from SLA calculation (simulate holiday during timer period). * Complete escalated task; verify completion time recorded and SLA compliance metric updated. Expected Results: SLA timer calculates KSA business days correctly. 80% warning triggers amber alert. 100% breach triggers red alert and escalation. Holidays excluded from calculation. Success Criteria: SLA calculation accuracy 100%; 80% warning fires correctly; 100% breach fires correctly; escalation routes to manager; holiday exclusion accurate; dashboard reflects breach.

Scenario 6: Evidence Collection — Mandatory Evidence at Stage 2 Preconditions: Workflow configured with mandatory evidence upload requirement at Stage 2 (“Technical Review”). Accepted formats: PDF, DOCX, XLSX. Max file size: 50MB. Steps: * Initiate workflow instance; complete Stage 1 (“Initial Review”) with approved decision. * Workflow progresses to Stage 2; verify task created for Technical Steward with evidence upload requirement flagged as mandatory. * Attempt to complete Stage 2 without uploading evidence; verify system blocks completion with error: “Mandatory evidence required. Please upload Technical Review Report (PDF, DOCX, or XLSX, max 50MB).” in bilingual display. * Upload file in invalid format (.exe); verify format validation error with allowed formats listed. * Upload file exceeding 50MB; verify size validation error with size limit displayed. * Upload valid PDF file (10MB, “Technical_Review_Report_v1.pdf”); verify upload successful with virus scan pass indicator. * Complete Stage 2 task; verify workflow progresses to Stage 3. * Verify evidence file linked to workflow instance with upload timestamp, uploader identity, and version tracking. * Download uploaded evidence; verify file integrity and content preserved. * Verify evidence appears in workflow audit trail with file reference and hash. Expected Results: Workflow blocks at Stage 2 without mandatory evidence. Invalid format and size rejected. Valid upload accepted. Evidence linked to workflow instance. Audit trail complete. Success Criteria: Block without evidence 100%; format validation 100%; size validation 100%; virus scan complete; evidence linkage 100%; audit trail accurate; file integrity preserved.

Scenario 7: Audit Trail Completeness — 10-Action Workflow Preconditions: Workflow instance initiated and executed through 10 distinct actions by 3 different actors. Steps: * Initiate workflow instance (Action 1: Initiation by DMO Admin). * Task assigned to Business Steward (Action 2: Assignment). * Business Steward approves with comment (Action 3: Approval). * Task routed to Technical Steward (Action 4: Routing). * Technical Steward uploads evidence (Action 5: Evidence Upload). * Technical Steward approves (Action 6: Approval). * Task routed to Security Reviewer (Action 7: Routing). * Security Reviewer requests clarification (Action 8: Rejection/Clarification). * Business Steward resubmits with updated info (Action 9: Resubmission). * Security Reviewer approves; workflow completes (Action 10: Final Approval/Completion). * Export workflow audit trail; verify all 10 actions logged with: actor identity (username + role), action type, timestamp (with timezone), before/after state, IP address, and session ID. * Verify audit trail sorted chronologically with sequence numbers. * Verify audit trail hash chain: each entry contains hash of previous entry, ensuring tamper evidence. * Verify audit trail export produces PDF with digital signature equivalent and CSV for analysis. * Verify audit trail accessible to auditors (read-only) with search and filter capability. Expected Results: All 10 actions logged with complete attribution. Audit trail sorted and hash-chained. Export produces PDF and CSV. Auditor access functional. Success Criteria: 10/10 actions logged; actor identity accurate; timestamp accurate; hash chain intact; export functional; auditor access read-only.

Scenario 8: Workflow Dashboard — 20 Case Execution Preconditions: Workflow dashboard configured. 20 workflow instances of mixed types initiated with varying statuses: 5 completed, 8 active, 4 overdue, 3 escalated. Steps: * Execute 20 workflow cases across 3 workflow types (Metadata Certification, DQ Remediation, Access Review) with varying durations and outcomes. * Allow 5 cases to complete within SLA; 4 cases to breach SLA (overdue); 3 cases to trigger escalation. * Navigate to Workflow Dashboard; verify metrics displayed: + Total Active Cases: 8 (correct) + Completed Today: count of today’s completions + Overdue Tasks: 4 (correct) + Escalated Cases: 3 (correct) + Average Completion Time: calculated correctly for completed cases + SLA Compliance Rate: percentage of on-time completions * Click “Overdue Tasks” metric; verify drill-down shows 4 specific overdue cases with case IDs, assignees, overdue duration, and current stage. * Click bottleneck analysis; verify stage with highest average dwell time identified. * Click “Tasks by Assignee”; verify workload distribution across stewards. * Click individual assignee; verify their task list with status, due dates, and priority. * Verify dashboard refreshes every 60 seconds with real-time data. * Verify trend charts show case volume and completion time trends over 7-day period. Expected Results: Dashboard metrics match actual case statuses. Drill-down functional from summary to detail. Bottleneck analysis identifies slowest stage. Real-time refresh active. Success Criteria: Dashboard metrics 100% accurate; drill-down functional; bottleneck identification correct; refresh < 60s; trend charts populated; workload distribution accurate. 5. Implementation Roadmap (Business-Focused 4-Phase Methodology) Note: This section replaces the previous technical-only 4-release roadmap with a comprehensive business-focused implementation methodology aligned to the Data Stewardship Program (DSP) framework. The new methodology prioritizes organizational readiness, governance body establishment, and business value delivery over technical feature delivery. Technical capabilities are still included but sequenced to support business outcomes. The DGOP v3.0 implementation follows a proven 4-phase methodology that has been successfully deployed across government entities worldwide. Each phase builds organizational capability while incrementally deploying technical functionality. The methodology aligns with SDAIA NDI assessment cycles, DGA NORA requirements, and the NDI maturity model (Initial → Defined → Activated → Enabled → Leading). Methodology Overview: Phase Duration Focus Key Outcomes Organization Maturity Phase 1: Foundation Months 1–3 Governance setup, charter, bodies, staffing DGSC established, Charter approved, DGPO staffed, tools selected Initial → Defined Phase 2: Pilot Months 4–6 1–2 domains, first stewards, initial workflows First BDEs defined, Domain Council active, 5–10 stewards trained, initial DQ rules Defined → Activated Phase 3: Expansion Months 7–12 5–8 domains, 100+ BDEs, all steward types Full steward coverage, certification program active, all DQ dimensions, MDM operational Activated → Enabled Phase 4: Optimization Year 2+ All domains, advanced analytics, automation, CoP Self-service catalog, automated DQ, predictive analytics, thriving community Enabled → Leading 6.1 Phase 1: Foundation (Months 1–3) Theme: Establish governance infrastructure, organizational readiness, and technical foundation. Objectives: Establish Data Governance Steering Committee (DGSC) with Deputy Minister/CEO sponsorship Draft, review, and approve the foundational Data Governance Charter with all 8 mandatory elements Staff the Data Governance Program Office (DGPO) per entity sizing guidance (Small: 2–3 FTEs; Medium: 4–6; Large: 8–12) Select and procure DGOP platform and supporting tools via structured 7-criteria evaluation and 6-step selection process Select 1–2 pilot domains based on business priority, data maturity, and steward availability Deploy core DGOP infrastructure (Administration, Ownership & Stewardship Registry, Workflow Engine) in DEV/TEST Establish ESC (Executive Steering Committee), Data Governance Board, and Data Stewardship Council structures Define decision rights matrix with time-boxed escalation paths (30/14/7/5 days) Critical Success Factors: # Success Factor Measurement 1 Executive sponsorship secured and sustained DGSC meets monthly; Tool Selection Framework (NEW) Note: This section is new in the enhanced volume. It provides a structured methodology for selecting and procuring the technology tools that support the DGOP ecosystem. The framework is adapted from the Data Stewardship Program’s proven tool selection methodology. The DGOP ecosystem requires integration with multiple specialized tools across six categories: Metadata Repository, Data Catalog, DQ Tool, Data Profiling Tool, Collaboration Platform, and Workflow Engine. While DGOP provides the governance orchestration layer, these tools provide the underlying data management capabilities. Selecting the right tools is critical to implementation success. 8.1 7 Weighted Evaluation Criteria Each tool is evaluated against 7 weighted criteria. Evaluation teams score each criterion on a 1–5 scale, then apply weights to calculate a weighted score. The total maximum score is 500 points. # Criterion Weight Description Scoring Guidance 1 Functional Fit 25% (125 pts) Degree to which the tool meets the entity’s functional requirements across all use cases 5=Exceeds all requirements; 4=Meets all; 3=Meets most with minor gaps; 2=Significant gaps; 1=Major gaps 2 Ease of Use 20% (100 pts) User experience for stewards, business users, and administrators; learning curve; UI quality 5=Excellent UX, minimal training; 4=Good UX, short learning curve; 3=Adequate; 2=Poor UX, extensive training; 1=Unusable without expert support 3 Integration Capability 20% (100 pts) API quality, connector availability, event support, DGOP integration ease, standards compliance 5=Native DGOP connector, REST API, Kafka events; 4=REST API + webhook; 3=REST API only; 2=Limited API; 1=No API 4 Scalability & Performance 10% (50 pts) Ability to handle projected data volumes, user counts, and throughput with acceptable performance 5=Exceeds 5-year projections; 4=Meets 5-year; 3=Meets 3-year; 2=Meets 1-year only; 1=Below requirements 5 Vendor Stability & Support 10% (50 pts) Vendor financial health, KSA presence, support quality, roadmap commitment, reference customers 5=Strong KSA presence, 3+ gov refs, 5+ year roadmap; 4=Regional presence, 1+ gov ref; 3=Global only; 2=Startup/uncertain; 1=High risk 6 Implementation & Deployment 10% (50 pts) Implementation complexity, time-to-value, KSA cloud deployment options, CITC certification status 5=CITC-certified, < 4 weeks deploy, KSA cloud native; 4=KSA deployable, < 8 weeks; 3=Deployable with effort; 2=Complex deployment; 1=Not KSA-ready 7 Training & Documentation 5% (25 pts) Quality of documentation, training materials, certification programs, Arabic language support 5=Bilingual docs, video training, certification program; 4=Bilingual docs, training available; 3=English only, good docs; 2=Limited docs; 1=Poor documentation Minimum Thresholds: Total weighted score must be ≥ 350/500 (70%) for serious consideration Functional Fit score must be ≥ 3.5/5 (minimum viable functional coverage) Integration Capability score must be ≥ 3.0/5 (must have usable API) Vendor Stability score must be ≥ 2.5/5 (vendor must be viable) Scorecard Template: Tool Category Functional Fit (25%) Ease of Use (20%) Integration (20%) Scalability (10%) Vendor (10%) Implementation (10%) Training (5%) Total Metadata Repository Data Catalog DQ Tool Profiling Tool MDM Hub Collaboration Platform 8.2 6-Step Selection Process Step 1: Requirements Definition (2–3 weeks) Assemble cross-functional evaluation team: DMO Head, Technical Architect, Data Steward representative, IT Procurement, Security Officer Document functional requirements per tool category using MoSCoW prioritization (Must/Should/Could/Won’t) Define non-functional requirements: performance, scalability, security, KSA cloud, CITC certification Document integration requirements with DGOP and existing enterprise systems Produce: Requirements Document per tool category Step 2: Request for Information (RFI) (2–3 weeks) Issue RFI to 8–12 vendors per tool category (include SDAIA-preferred vendors where applicable) Request: company profile, product overview, KSA/customer references, architecture description, high-level pricing Evaluate RFI responses against initial requirements; shortlist 4–6 vendors per category Produce: RFI Evaluation Report with vendor shortlist Step 3: Request for Proposal (RFP) (3–4 weeks) Issue detailed RFP to shortlisted vendors RFP includes: functional requirements checklist, scenario-based demonstration script, technical architecture questionnaire, security compliance requirements (NCA ECC, PDPL), implementation timeline, TCO pricing template Require vendor response structure: Executive Summary, Functional Response, Technical Architecture, Security & Compliance, Implementation Approach, Pricing, References Produce: RFP Package Issued, Vendor Response Evaluation Matrix Step 4: Demonstration & Evaluation (3–4 weeks) Schedule structured demonstrations with 3–4 finalist vendors per category Demonstration script covers 5 mandatory scenarios and 3 optional scenarios specific to the entity’s use cases Evaluation team scores each vendor using the 7 weighted criteria scorecard Reference checks conducted with 2–3 KSA government customers per finalist Produce: Demonstration Evaluation Report, Reference Check Summary, Weighted Scorecard Results Step 5: Proof of Concept (POC) (4–6 weeks) Invite top 2 vendors per category to POC POC scope: deploy in entity TEST environment, integrate with DGOP DEV, execute 3 success criteria scenarios POC success criteria defined in advance with measurable outcomes Technical team evaluates: integration scoring with sensitivity analysis (what-if on criteria weights) Risk assessment: vendor stability, lock-in risk, exit strategy Negotiate contract terms: licensing, SLA, support, implementation services, training, renewal Obtain DGSC approval for selected vendors Execute contracts and issue purchase orders Produce: Tool Selection Final Report with recommendation, Signed Contracts, Implementation Best Practices & Common Pitfalls to Avoid (NEW) Note: This section is new in the enhanced volume. It consolidates implementation best practices from the Data Stewardship Program framework and lessons learned from DGOP deployments across KSA government entities. 9.1 Top 10 Implementation Best Practices 1. Secure Executive Sponsorship Before Any Technical Work Executive sponsorship is the single most important success factor. The DGSC must be chaired at Deputy Minister or CEO level with monthly meeting cadence. Without sustained executive commitment, governance programs lose priority when operational pressures arise. Secure a ministerial decree or formal mandate where possible, aligning DGOP with NDI compliance requirements to create regulatory urgency. Evidence Required: DGSC meeting minutes showing sponsor attendance ≥ 80%; signed Charter with executive approval; budget allocation confirmed. 2. Invest Heavily in the Foundation Phase Organizations that rush through Phase 1 (Foundation) experience 3x more issues in later phases. The Charter, DGPO staffing, and governance body establishment are prerequisites for sustainable governance. A well-crafted Charter prevents months of organizational friction. Fully staffed DGPO ensures consistent program momentum. Evidence Required: Charter approved within 60 days; DGPO staffed before pilot begins; governance bodies meeting regularly before tool deployment. 3. Select Pilot Domains Strategically — Not Conveniently The most convenient domains (smallest, least political) rarely produce the business value needed to sustain the program. Select pilot domains based on: (a) business criticality, (b) data maturity, (c) steward availability, (d) visible business pain points, and (e) executive sponsor influence. Successful pilots in high-visibility domains generate organizational momentum. Evidence Required: Pilot domain selection matrix with scoring; business owner commitment letters; visible DQ issues detected and remediated within first 60 days. 4. Define Business Data Elements Before Technical Implementation BDEs are the foundational building blocks of data governance. Every DQ rule, metadata entry, lineage trace, and classification decision references BDEs. Invest in thorough BDE discovery workshops with business subject matter experts before configuring technical rules. A well-defined BDE with business glossary entry, technical mapping, and quality rules is worth more than 100 automated rules without business context. Evidence Required: ≥ 20 BDEs per pilot domain with complete business glossary entries; BDE definitions validated by business owners; BDEs linked to DQ rules and metadata assets. 5. Invest in Steward Training from Day One — Not as an Afterthought Stewards who receive comprehensive training within 30 days of onboarding are 4x more productive than those trained ad-hoc. Training is not a cost center — it is a force multiplier. The 4-tier curriculum ensures progressive skill building. Certification creates career pathways that retain talent. Budget 15–20% of total program cost for training and certification. Evidence Required: ≥ 90% of stewards complete Tier 1 within 30 days of onboarding; certification program active; steward productivity metrics improving month-over-month. 6. Build a Thriving Community of Practice Technical governance tools solve operational problems; communities solve human problems. A thriving CoP reduces steward isolation, accelerates knowledge sharing, creates mentorship pathways, and sustains program momentum beyond DGPO involvement. The CoP must be launched early (Phase 2) and nurtured actively through Phase 3. Evidence Required: ≥ 50 active CoP members within 6 months; ≥ 10 mentorship pairs active; member-generated content exceeds DGPO-generated content; monthly meetups with ≥ 60% attendance. 7. Implement Severity-Based SLAs from the Start Not all data quality issues are equal. A flat SLA framework creates alert fatigue and causes stewards to miss genuinely critical issues. The 4-severity framework (Critical/High/Medium/Low) with per-stage timers ensures appropriate urgency. Critical issues (breach of sensitive data) require 4-hour triage; Low issues (cosmetic formatting) can wait 5 days. Evidence Required: Severity-based SLA framework configured; Critical issue MTTR < 8 hours; stewards can articulate severity definitions; escalation paths tested monthly. 8. Integrate Privacy by Design into SDLC from Day One Retrofitting privacy controls after system deployment costs 10x more than embedding them during development. The Privacy by Design SDLC integration ensures every new system, enhancement, and data processing activity passes privacy review before deployment. Start with one development team, demonstrate value, then expand. Evidence Required: 100% of new projects pass all 5 SDLC phase gates; DPIA auto-creation for high-risk projects; privacy risk scores visible in project dashboard; development team satisfaction ≥ 70%. 9. Measure and Communicate Business Value Continuously Governance programs survive on demonstrated business value, not compliance mandates. Track and communicate: data quality improvement (reduced defect rates), operational efficiency (faster report generation), risk reduction (fewer breaches, faster remediation), and compliance readiness (NDI maturity scores). Monthly value dashboards keep executive sponsors engaged. Evidence Required: Monthly value dashboard with ≥ 5 KPIs; executive briefings monthly; business value metrics improving quarter-over-quarter; stakeholder satisfaction survey ≥ 4.0/5. 10. Plan for Continuous Improvement, Not Just Deployment DGOP deployment is not a destination — it is the beginning of a governance journey. The continuous improvement program with 6-month assessment cycles, gap heat maps, and improvement planning ensures the program evolves with organizational needs. Maturity assessment across 4 dimensions (Compliance, Awareness, Roles, Value) provides holistic progress tracking. Evidence Required: 6-month assessment cycles scheduled and executed; gap heat maps produced; improvement plans implemented; maturity progression documented (Initial → Defined → Activated → Enabled → Leading). 9.2 Top 10 Common Pitfalls to Avoid 1. Starting with Technology Instead of Organization Pitfall: Deploying DGOP before establishing governance bodies, approving the Charter, or staffing the DGPO. Impact: Tools configured without organizational context; stewards unclear on roles; workflows route to non-existent approvers. Prevention: Complete Phase 1 (Foundation) before any PROD deployment. Charter first, people second, tools third. 2. Defining Too Many BDEs Too Quickly Pitfall: Attempting to define 500+ BDEs in the first month, resulting in shallow definitions without business validation. Impact: BDEs rejected by business owners; DQ rules based on incorrect definitions; loss of stakeholder confidence. Prevention: Start with 20–50 high-value BDEs per domain; deep definition over broad coverage; validate with business owners before technical implementation. 3. Neglecting Operational and Project Steward Roles Pitfall: Focusing only on Business and Technical Stewards while ignoring Operational Stewards (front-line quality) and Project Stewards (temporary governance). Impact: Data quality issues persist at point of entry; new projects launch without governance; 80% of DQ issues originate at data creation. Prevention: Include Operational Stewards in Phase 2; activate Project Stewards before major IT initiatives; train front-line staff on data quality importance. 4. Setting Unrealistic Timelines Under Executive Pressure Pitfall: Committing to “full governance in 6 months” to satisfy executive urgency. Impact: Rushed implementation with poor adoption; superficial BDE definitions; untrained stewards; program failure within 12 months. Prevention: Set realistic timelines: Foundation 3 months, Pilot 3 months, Expansion 6 months, Optimization 12+ months. Educate executives on phased approach value. 5. Insufficient Change Management and Communication Pitfall: Treating DGOP as a technical project rather than an organizational change initiative. Impact: Business users resist new processes; stewards revert to informal practices; governance becomes “checkbox compliance.” Prevention: Dedicate 10–15% of budget to change management; regular communication cadence (weekly updates, monthly town halls); celebrate early wins; involve stakeholders in design decisions. 6. Poor DQ Rule Design — Quantity Over Quality Pitfall: Deploying 1,000+ DQ rules in the first month without business context or threshold tuning. Impact: 90% of alerts are false positives; stewards overwhelmed; rules disabled within 3 months; loss of confidence in DQ framework. Prevention: Start with 10–20 high-confidence rules; tune thresholds with business input; expand incrementally; maintain ≥ 80% true positive rate. 7. Ignoring Data Profiling Before Rule Definition Pitfall: Defining DQ rules based on assumptions rather than actual data analysis. Impact: Rules don’t match data reality; missed quality issues; rules based on edge cases rather than patterns. Prevention: Profile all critical datasets before rule definition; use profiling anomalies to inform rule design; re-profile quarterly to detect drift. 8. Inadequate Training — Assuming Users Will “Figure It Out” Pitfall: Providing a 2-hour overview and expecting stewards to become productive. Impact: Stewards use only 10% of DGOP capabilities; workarounds proliferate; support tickets escalate; program value not realized. Prevention: Mandate 4-tier curriculum; provide hands-on labs; assign mentors to new stewards; track training completion as performance metric. 9. Treating Classification as a One-Time Exercise Pitfall: Classifying assets once and never reviewing, resulting in stale classifications as data evolves. Impact: Confidential data treated as Internal; PUBLIC data contains PII; PDPL compliance gaps; breach risk. Prevention: Annual classification review in compliance calendar; automatic re-classification triggers on schema change; quarterly sampling audit. 10. Lack of Executive Engagement After Launch Pitfall: Executive sponsor attends launch event then disengages; DGSC meetings cancelled due to “scheduling conflicts.” Impact: Program loses priority; budget cuts; key staff seconded elsewhere; governance atrophies to checkbox compliance. Prevention: Monthly executive dashboard with value metrics; quarterly DGSC with mandatory attendance; tie governance metrics to executive performance objectives; annual governance summit with ministerial participation. 10. It provides three tailored implementation scenarios for common entity situations: Greenfield (no existing governance), Brownfield (some governance exists), and Transformation/M&A (rapid deployment during organizational change).
